title: Value-Based Delivery & Benefits
summary: The 2026 exam reframes success around delivering value worth the effort - not just hitting scope, schedule, and cost. Value-based delivery is now its own Process task.

sections.heading: Value is the point
sections.body:
PMI reframed project success: a project succeeds when it delivers value worth the effort and expense, not merely when it finishes on time and on budget. The 2026 outline makes 'help ensure value-based delivery' a distinct Process task.

So when scope, schedule, and cost pull against delivering real value, the value-focused choice is usually the better answer.
sections.tip: On the exam, prefer the option that protects or increases stakeholder value over the one that just defends the original plan.

sections.heading: Identify value with stakeholders
sections.body: Value is defined by stakeholders, not assumed by the team. Work with key stakeholders to identify the value components that matter - the outcomes, benefits, and measures of success behind the deliverables - so the team builds what is actually worth building.
sections.tip: If the team and stakeholders disagree on what 'value' means, align that first - before prioritising or building.

sections.heading: Prioritise by value
sections.body:
With limited time and money, sequence work so the highest-value items come first. Common prioritisation methods include:

- MoSCoW (must / should / could / won't)
- cost of delay
- the 100-point method

Stakeholder feedback continually reshapes the order.

This is why agile and incremental approaches pull high-value features forward instead of treating all scope as equal.
sections.tip: When asked what to build first, choose the highest-value, feedback-informed item - not the easiest or the first one written down.

sections.heading: Deliver value incrementally
sections.body:
Don't make stakeholders wait until the end to see value. Assess where work can be delivered in increments, release usable outcomes early, and use the feedback to steer. A minimum viable product and regular demonstrations turn delivery into a series of value checkpoints.

Early delivery also de-risks the project: you learn what truly delivers value before spending the whole budget.
sections.tip: Favour delivering a smaller useful increment now over a perfect complete solution much later.

sections.heading: Measure benefits
sections.body:
You can only 'ensure' value if you measure it. Verify a measurement system is in place to track benefits, with clear metrics and a benefits owner. Some benefits appear during delivery; many are realised after handover, so benefits tracking extends into operations.

A benefits management plan links each deliverable to the outcome it is meant to produce.
sections.tip: If a benefit has no metric and no owner, it won't be realised - flag that gap.

sections.heading: Re-check value throughout
sections.body: Value is not a one-time business-case exercise. Examine business value throughout the project: as the environment, priorities, or assumptions change, a once-valuable feature may no longer be worth it. Be willing to re-prioritise, descope, or even recommend stopping work that no longer delivers value.
sections.tip: If the scenario shows the business case has eroded, the strong answer revisits value and options - not blindly finishing the original scope.

source: Aligned with the PMP Examination Content Outline - July 2026 (Process: help ensure value-based delivery) and PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition (value delivery).
